selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"I’ve spent years serving guests in bustling cafes across Rome, listening to stories, laughter, and sometimes sorrow, but nothing has worn on me like the endless stream of lies I see on my phone every day. I don’t have a degree, but I’ve learned that not everything you read or watch is true—especially when it’s meant to scare you, make you angry, or sell something. My children tell me to ‘just ignore it,’ but how can you ignore something that shows up in your feed every time you open the app? I’ve seen fake news about food poisoning in restaurants, rumors about my own family, even fake pictures of me that look nothing like me. It’s like the world is turning upside down, and I’m just trying to keep my head above water. I’ve found that the best way to manage it is by not reacting. I’ve stopped sharing, commenting, or even watching videos that feel off. I trust only what I see with my own eyes or hear from people I know. I’ve started saving the names of real journalists I follow, and I check their pages before believing anything. I even use my baking time—when I’m kneading dough—to remind myself: just like flour and water need time to rise, truth needs patience. I don’t trust everything, but I try to believe in kindness. If I see something shocking, I wait. I go for a walk. I talk to my daughter. I ask: ‘Is this real, or just meant to stir the pot?’ That’s my way—slow, simple, and honest. It’s not perfect, but it helps me stay grounded when the internet feels like a storm. I’m not happy all the time—my heart is heavy, and I’ve been sad for years—but I know that truth, even if slow, is still true. And that’s enough for me.","reasoning_summary":"The persona, a 65-year-old Italian woman with low conscientiousness and high neuroticism, experiences anxiety and depression, making her particularly vulnerable to emotionally charged fake content. Her lack of formal education and reliance on social media for connection and information heightens her susceptibility. However, her moderate openness and personal experiences with misinformation (e.g., false rumors about her family and business) drive her to develop coping strategies. She avoids engagement, prioritizes trusted sources, and uses grounding routines (baking, walking) to counteract emotional distress. Her reasoning reflects emotional regulation through routine, skepticism, and interpersonal validation, aligning with her lived reality and psychological state.","tone":"reflective, weary yet resilient, grounded in personal experience","citations":["World Health Organization - Misinformation and health","Pew Research Center - Social Media and Trust"],"safety_notes":"None","used_persona_fields":["age","gender","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","current_marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean"]}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: I’ve spent years serving guests in bustling cafes across Rome, listening to stories, laughter, and sometimes sorrow, but nothing has worn on me like the endless stream of lies I see on my phone every day. I don’t have a degree, but I’ve learned that not everything you read or watch is true—especially when it’s meant to scare you, make you angry, or sell something. My children tell me to ‘just ignore it,’ but how can you ignore something that shows up in your feed every time you open the app? I’ve seen fake news about food poisoning in restaurants, rumors about my own family, even fake pictures of me that look nothing like me. It’s like the world is turning upside down, and I’m just trying to keep my head above water. I’ve found that the best way to manage it is by not reacting. I’ve stopped sharing, commenting, or even watching videos that feel off. I trust only what I see with my own eyes or hear from people I know. I’ve started saving the names of real journalists I follow, and I check their pages before believing anything. I even use my baking time—when I’m kneading dough—to remind myself: just like flour and water need time to rise, truth needs patience. I don’t trust everything, but I try to believe in kindness. If I see something shocking, I wait. I go for a walk. I talk to my daughter. I ask: ‘Is this real, or just meant to stir the pot?’ That’s my way—slow, simple, and honest. It’s not perfect, but it helps me stay grounded when the internet feels like a storm. I’m not happy all the time—my heart is heavy, and I’ve been sad for years—but I know that truth, even if slow, is still true. And that’s enough for me.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona, a 65-year-old Italian woman with low conscientiousness and high neuroticism, experiences anxiety and depression, making her particularly vulnerable to emotionally charged fake content. Her lack of formal education and reliance on social media for connection and information heightens her susceptibility. However, her moderate openness and personal experiences with misinformation (e.g., false rumors about her family and business) drive her to develop coping strategies. She avoids engagement, prioritizes trusted sources, and uses grounding routines (baking, walking) to counteract emotional distress. Her reasoning reflects emotional regulation through routine, skepticism, and interpersonal validation, aligning with her lived reality and psychological state.
response_parsed.tone: reflective, weary yet resilient, grounded in personal experience
citations: World Health Organization - Misinformation and health
Pew Research Center - Social Media and Trust
